vulnerabilities.name: run-shell-injection
vulnerabilities.message: Using variable interpolation `${{...}}` with `github` context data in a `run:` step could allow an attacker to inject their own code into the runner. This would allow them to steal secrets and code. `github` context data can have arbitrary user input and should be treated as untrusted. Instead, use an intermediate environment variable with `env:` to store the data and use the environment variable in the `run:` script. Be sure to use double-quotes the environment variable, like this: "$ENVVAR".
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-78: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command ('OS Command Injection')

vulnerabilities.name: tainted-filename
vulnerabilities.message: File name based on user input risks server-side request forgery.

vulnerabilities.name: phpinfo-use
vulnerabilities.message: The 'phpinfo' function may reveal sensitive information about your environment.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-200: Exposure of Sensitive Information to an Unauthorized Actor

vulnerabilities.name: exec-use
vulnerabilities.message: Executing non-constant commands. This can lead to command injection.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

vulnerabilities.name: tainted-sql-string
vulnerabilities.message: User data flows into this manually-constructed SQL string. User data can be safely inserted into SQL strings using prepared statements or an object-relational mapper (ORM). Manually-constructed SQL strings is a possible indicator of SQL injection, which could let an attacker steal or manipulate data from the database. Instead, use prepared statements (`$mysqli->prepare("INSERT INTO test(id, label) VALUES (?, ?)");`) or a safe library.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-89: Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an SQL Command ('SQL Injection')

vulnerabilities.name: php-exec-variable
vulnerabilities.message: PHP exec/system/shell_exec called with a variable argument — potential command injection. Verify the variable cannot contain user-controlled data. Use escapeshellarg() for any user-supplied arguments and prefer PHP built-in functions over shell calls.

vulnerabilities.name: exec-use
vulnerabilities.message: Executing non-constant commands. This can lead to command injection.
vulnerabilities.cwe: CWE-94: Improper Control of Generation of Code ('Code Injection')

vulnerabilities.name: php-include-variable
vulnerabilities.message: PHP include/require with a variable argument — potential file inclusion if the variable contains user-controlled data (LFI/RFI). Ensure the variable value is derived from a strict allowlist and never from $_GET/$_POST/$_REQUEST.

vulnerabilities.name: eval-dynamic-string-js
vulnerabilities.message: eval() called with a non-literal expression. If the argument can be influenced by user input, this is a critical code injection vulnerability. Remove eval() or replace with JSON.parse() / a safe expression parser.

vulnerabilities.name: php-header-redirect
vulnerabilities.message: PHP header() Location redirect uses user-controlled input — open redirect. Validate the URL against a whitelist of allowed paths before redirecting.

Instead, use prepared statements (`$mysqli->prepare("INSERT INTO test(id, label) VALUES (?, ?)");`) or a safe library.

vulnerabilities.name: php-html-buffer-superglobal
vulnerabilities.message: Reflected XSS — $_GET/$_POST/$_REQUEST concatenated into HTML buffer without htmlspecialchars(). Note: mysqli_real_escape_string() prevents SQL injection but does NOT prevent XSS — HTML encoding is separate.
